speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of an interview with Regina Clipper, May 17, 1995 in New York. Interviewer, Juliette Halpern. You were talking about being on your way back to Radom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're sitting in the-- in the shed and wait. As we entered the shed, I don't know what happened, but I fainted. And she got hysterical. She thought I had died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But then I woke up. And, and we went to-- we crept through the ghetto. It was dark. And we watched out nobody should see us. And we looked for an opening. It was fenced in. And we found-- we did find where one board was missing. It was about this big. And to this day, I don't know how we got through there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had another friend who also used to work for the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I knew where she was in the ghetto. So we went there. And she says, oh my god, you're back? I was like, yes. She said, come on. Hide under the bed because they search-- the Jewish police, you know? They had to, I mean the were-- so we slept under the bed for the night. Explain that. What do you mean, they had to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had the Germans sitting there. And when he said, I want you to watch out that nobody should bring in something or come in the ghetto. So they had to go. I don't know if they would have--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they were appointed by the Germans to function like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if they would have reported us. I don't think so. But she said, better safe than sorry. You hide. And during the day everybody went out to work. We couldn't be in the ghetto-- I mean inside. So we hid in the cellars. And I never thought I would be afraid of rats. I mean, I, I knew I was going to be afraid of rats, but when you have to, you have to, you-- use-- even if there are rats. And by some coincidence I got a job in the hospital, in the infirmary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I cleaned. I made the beds. I did the laundry. I, I helped the nurses. I fed the people-- what you do in a hospital, a very primitive. So then something happened. They brought in-- at night, if somebody was shot in the street-- they'd escaped the ghetto or a transport when somebody jumped from the train-- they brought the dead people into this infirmary. And one night-- I worked nights that week. The, the doctor in charge, and nurse, were busy with patient told me that I should open the, the doors-- it was like a gate, a big wooden gate-- because they're bringing in the corpses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what corpses? Well, that apparently people jumped off the train, and they were shot. And we have to bury them. So they brought in the corpses, opened up the gate. And they put the corpses in the alley. There was like an alley. And they said to me, see, maybe it's somebody is still alive. I mean, I was a young girl and you know, it was the first time. I went to look at the corpses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the corpses were all from Westerbork, Holland. All the men, beautifully dressed with name tags, shot in the head, mostly in the neck because the face was-- I could see the faces. Children, babies, mothers with babies, and they were all killed. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] [DRINKING] They were not my relatives, but, you know, it struck me especially because I lived in Holland, and I lost people like that. And, and in the morning, they were driven to the cemetery and buried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I got sick. And I had-- I got typhus. I was very sick. And we had a doctor. He was the head doctor of the whole ghetto. And he came in and looked at the-- at the sick. And he came to my bed. And he said, who are you? I was already a little better, and said, my name is Regina Rosensweig. He says, Rosensweig? You have an uncle Chaim? I say, yes, but he's in Palestine. Yeah, he said, he was my best friend. So he sent down food and-- I'll never forget-- a bottle of red wine. And, and he saw to it that I, I got well. And I got well. And then came '43-- it was-- that was in '43 already. It came towards the summer, and we heard that they're going to liquidate the ghetto, completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also during that time, they brought a little baby boy that a mother had left on the street. Somebody should take care of him. They brought him to a hospital, and we took care of him and we named him Shulim. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so they liquidated the ghetto. And half of the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Shulim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shulim, well, I don't really know what happened to him. They liberate-- they liberated. They liquidated the ghetto, and one side went to the one ammunition factory and the other-- we went to Pionik. And I wanted to take this baby along. And this Dr. [? Shindolovich, ?] the head off the, the ghetto and the medical staff, took him away. He said, no, I'll take care of him. You go to the factory. And that's what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came-- we drove. It was about 40 miles or something, for-- no, little less because it took us a few hours, that's all, and on-- and on a track that went very slow. So we came to Pionik, and I didn't know anybody there. And the barrack that we were supposed to be in was not ready, had no roof. So I was sitting on a bench in front of the kitchen window, praying that somebody should throw something my way. And as I was sitting there, a blonde, beautiful blonde girl looked out the window. And she said to me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when did you come? I said, I came now, today. And I haven't got where to sleep. And I said, what, what are you doing? You're not Jewish. She says, oh yes I am-- blonde hair and blue eyes and very pretty. She says, my name is Hanka, and you've got a friend now. So she took me in-- their infirmary was very small. There was a bench, a wooden bench. She gave me a blanket, and something rolled up for my head. And I slept there. And she brought me something to eat. She worked in the kitchen, and she stole food and gave it around. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then the next day, we got into the barracks. And in this barrack was a woman whose husband had been shot with a lot of other intelligensia from the ghetto. And she was there with three children. She paid off a Ukraine SS man with all the jewelry that she had yet. And he let her go with the children. He told her to get-- to bury herself underneath the corpses. And she came back into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, she came to Pionik. And she was-- people knew her, though she didn't have to go to work, the factory, and the children were with her. And we all tried to play with the children after work. And so first, I worked. Well, we all had to go to work. First I worked, again, cleaning the streets, tending some, some garden for the SS, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unloading coal and stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I got a job in a-- where they make the ammunition itself, the, the powder, the-- what do you call it? Gun powder?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gun powder. So I got a job. The SS man told me I have to-- there was a wooden walk, a wooden plank from one building to another building. And a little-- like a cart-- a little wheel wagon barrel on rubber, on very-- and they put something in it, in a keg. And I had to deliver it to the different factories, the different buildings. And they said I shouldn't jar it. I didn't know what it was. And I did as I was told. And I don't know how I didn't blow up with it because it was nitroglycerin. And I worked there '40-- '40-- that was '44, in the summer of '44. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh yeah, one day there was an explosion in one of the factories, and people burned. And I saw a man coming down the road on fire, naked. All-- everything was burned off. And it was the most horrible thing. He was black from, from cha-- charcoal, a young man. He died. He died right away. And one day they hung three people because they had stole some alcohol--

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the factory. Yeah, we all had to assemble. And they hung them right then and there. In 1944 in July, we went to Auschwitz. We were loaded in the-- the whole-- the Russians came closer, and they took the whole camp. And we went to Auschwitz. We travelled quite a few days in packed wagons. And we got to Birkenau, and there was a selection on the ramp. And this one, and this one here. And the woman, the doctor's wife, was with three children-- the children were taken away. And when she wanted to follow the children, they beat her. And they were gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You witnessed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we went into the sauna, they select-- whenever we-- the whole group went into the s-- the showers. And then we knew already, you know, what-- but first they took us to shave our heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that the showers mean the gas. They shaved our heads, and they led us into the showers. And we stood there and water came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had expected?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had expected of the-- here, this is the end of the story, you know? So the showers. And then we got out. And there was a heap of clothing, you know? And we had to grab some kind of clothes. And whatever we put on. And you know how girls are? They look in the mirror? There is no mirror. There's a window pane. And I looked, and I turned around to look who was standing there behind me. I did not recognize myself. It was-- it's such a shock when you have hair and they shave it off. And then we were put into the barracks, the Auschwitz barracks. Six or seven people on one with nothing on there, I mean, wood and a blanket. I think there was a blanket. I don't know. It was terrible. And we were there a week. We were tattooed after a few days, the numbers. And every morning we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a number  on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here. A14948. And then we, we had to go for selection again-- this time nude, completely undressed. And we stood in rows, and not close together, but so that somebody could walk through the rows of women. So we were this one, this one, this one, this one. And from 5,000 women, they selected, I think, about 500. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they put us in stripes, you know, the striped clothing from Auschwitz. And put us--

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you in Auschwitz or in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau, Birkenau. And they put us on, on, on a, a truck. And they drove us to Hindenburg because we had worked in an ammunition factory. And that was an ammunition factory, too. So we worked there. We had to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there. We had barracks. And they were clean. They were like bunk beds. In every barr-- barrack, there were about, oh, 25 or so, 20. And we got a piece of bread in the morning and some-- something that resem-- should have resembled coffee, which wasn't. And then we got the soup where-- at work, a bowl of soup. And at night when we came home, we got another piece of bread. And I became a welder. They taught us how the to weld, electric welder, welding. And we made parts for airplanes. There were a few-- we were all women. There were a few French prisoners of war, men who did certain things that we were not supposed to do. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a 12 hour shift. We worked from 6:00 in the morning until 6:00 at night. And then we went to shower. And we had a SS woman that was watching us. She went with us to the showers. And she yelled and screamed and she talked and a very big, buxom blonde.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she talked to me about something, [GERMAN], whatever it was. And I aid to her, you have a strange accent for German, for a German. [GERMAN.] I should shut up. And when she said that, I said, oh, you're from Holland. Well, did I get it from Holland. God almighty. I, I-- the Dutch women that joined the Nazis, I guess, didn't want to be called Dutch. And she beat me up good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a, a, you know, a whip. And she went like this, twice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: On your back or in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my back. On my back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we worked at night, when we had the night shift, there was an old German soldier-- old, I mean, he was maybe 60-- that came to watch us. And he always fell asleep. And I told him he should watch out because the, the commandant from the camp comes to check sometimes. And if he sees him asleep, he'll get into trouble. Yeah, he said, he's going to send me to the Russian front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we watched. Even if he fell asleep, we used to watch him. He shouldn't get into trouble because he was good to us, and he brought us food. He stole it from the dogs, from the SS men. You know, whatever they didn't eat they gave to the dogs. And he took it away from the dogs. And he brought it to us-- potatoes, or some vegetables, or whatever, bread, or whatever it was. And we ate it there because he was afraid if you bring something in, they're going to ask what is that? is that? So in-- we had a doctor from Czechoslovakia, a woman. Very nice, and she was-- she was really terrific. And he had a little respect, the commandant, for her. So he-- she told him that we do need some kind of sugar, sugar or, or some kind of protein or something. So, so he let her have a little sugar for, for each of us. Then we got from the Red Cross what we really, desperately needed-- cigarettes. Everybody got two cigarettes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in our camp were gypsies. There were 30 gypsies that worked in the kitchen. So we sold the gypsy-- the, the cigarettes to the gypsies for a bowl of soup. But they finagled. They didn't give us the soup. They just took the cigarettes. So a friend of mine and I got very angry about that. They were prisoners, too. So we went and-- haggled with the cigarette. And they wanted to do it again. And I was so mad that when she held the soup like this, I banged the soup underneath, and it came right on her. And it was hot. But she wouldn't tell anybody. She went to infirmary and said she burned herself because she wasn't supposed to buy the cigarettes, and she wasn't supposed to have soup. Then the men came to Hindenburg-- another, across the way, also about a few hundred. And one of the girls that was in our camp was very beautiful. And she worked for the commandant, cleaned and cooked for him. And in this group of men was a young man whom she liked, or he liked her, because the, the windows from the commandant's apartment looked out on the men's camp. And she slipped him some food. Well, the commandant heard about it and did they get a beating, both of them-- horrendous, horrendous. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, anyway, in, in the winter of-- the end of '44, they liquidated. They rounded us up and put us in a wagon and drove us to a place. And we started to march through Hindenburg and through Gleiwitz. We marched for three days in the snow and ice. It was horrible. And all the people-- through civilian streets-- and all the people looked out the window. And nobody, nobody had a kind word. They were standing, and we were marching by. And nobody had a kind word, the Germans and the Poles. All you heard was Jude, Jude, Jude, you know, [GERMAN]. And when we arrived in Gleiwitz, back and forth, we marched. And they put us in wagons. And we went on the transport. And we didn't know where, back and forth. The trains went back and forth, and back and forth. And the wagons were open. There was ice on the wagons. And people got so sick. And we had to go to the bathroom. And they wouldn't stop, the Germans. And then they stopped. And you know, you become like an animal. You sit in the snow, and all these, these men standing and these soldiers, and, and we ate the snow and the ice, you know from the water--

speaker: interviewer
text: Icicles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Icicles. And back and forth, we finally landed in Dora, Nordhausen. We didn't know what kind of a camp this was, really. But we, we drove into the mountain and, there were prisoners that wore a green triangle. They were-- green triangle, were criminals, murderers. They were there. And political was a red triangle. And we had-- we had-- we had stripes. So we came there. And we went-- we were there for about a week, I think. And they loaded us into the wagons again. With-- the wagons must have transported graphite at sometime. We were came to Bergen-Belsen. We all were black, like coal. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they took us to the sauna, to the-- where we had to strip everything. And again, they shaved our heads. And we had showers. And then we got some clothes, whatever clothes. And they put us in barracks with some straw on the-- on the floor, on the cement floor. And that's where we sat. We got one piece of bread a day. And there was one woman, she came from Czechoslovakia. She was Czechoslovakian. Her husband, I spoke to her and her husband. And she just sat there. She didn't wash herself. She didn't get up. She didn't even walk out. We could walk out. It was a camp. She just sat there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pause now if--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
